elements.sent-2411-001: We study the expected number of solutions of a system of identically distributed exponential sums with centered Gaussian coefficient and arbitrary variance.
elements.sent-2411-002: We use the Adler and Taylor theory of Gaussian random fields to identify a moment map which allows to express the expected number of solution as an integral over the Newton polytope, in analogy with the Bernstein Khovanskii Kushnirenko Theorem.
elements.sent-2411-003: We apply this result to study the monotonicity of the expected number of solution with respect to the support of the exponential sum in an open set.
elements.sent-2411-004: We find that, when a point is added in the support in the interior of the Newton polytope there exists an open sets where the expected number of solutions decreases, answering negatively to a local version of a conjecture by Bürgisser.
elements.sent-2411-005: When the point added in the support is far enough away from the Newton polytope we show that, in dimension $1$, the number of solutions increases everywhere, while in dimension $>1$ there is an unbounded open set where the number of solution decreases.
elements.sent-2411-006: We also prove some new lower bounds for the Aronszajn multiplication of exponential sums.

elements.section-2411-005: Complex Gaussian Exponential sums and the BKK Theorem
elements.sent-2411-755: The aim of this section is to give a proof of the BKK Theorem and its generalization \cref{mainthm:Kaza , using Gaussain Random Fields and our framework.}
elements.sent-2411-756: Let us start with some definitions.
elements.sent-2411-757: A Gaussian vector $\xi\randin\bbC$ is called a standard complex Gaussian if it is $U(1)$ invariant and satisfy $\bbE\xi=0$ and $\bbE[|\xi|^2]=1$.
elements.sent-2411-758: Equivalently, $\xi$ is distributed as $\tfrac{1 {\sqrt{2 (\xi^R+i \xi^I)$ where $\xi^R,\xi^I\randin \bbR$ are standard (real) Gaussian variables.}}}
elements.sent-2411-759: Moreover, we call $\xi\randin\bbC^n$ a standard complex Gaussian vector if it is distributed as $(\xi_1,\ldots,\xi_n)$ where $\xi_1,\ldots,\xi_n\randin \bbC$ are iid standard complex Gaussians.
elements.sent-2411-760: Finally, a (centered) complex Gaussian vector is the image of a standard complex Gaussian vector by a $\bbC$-linear map.
elements.sent-2411-761: Note that, when identifying $\bbC\cong\bbR^2$ a standard complex Gaussian is not a standard Gaussian vector of $\bbR^2$ because of the coefficient $1/\sqrt{2}$.
elements.sent-2411-762: More precisely, a standard complex Gaussian vector in $\bbC^n$ has density $z\mapsto \frac{1 {\pi^n e^{|z|^2 .$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-763: We fix $A\subset \bbC^n$ finite and as before write $P:=\conv(A)$ for the Newton polytope.
elements.sent-2411-764: We now want to study the complex Gaussian exponential sum: \begin{equation}\Exp^\bbC(z):=\sum_{a\in A}\xi_a f^\bbC_a(z)\end{equation} where $\xi_a\randin\bbC$ are independent standard complex Gaussians and $f^\bbC_a(z):=\alpha_a e^{(a,z)}$ with $(\cdot,\cdot):\bbC^n\times\bbC^n\to \bbC$ is the standard Hermitian form and $\alpha_a>0$.
elements.sent-2411-765: Note that, if $\xi\randin \bbC$ is a standard Gaussian and $c\in \bbC$.
elements.sent-2411-766: Then $c\xi$ is distributed as $|c|\xi$ thus the fact that we take real positve coefficients in the exponential sum is not a restriction.
elements.sent-2411-767: As before, we define the following quantities for all $z\in \bbC^n$: \begin{equation}\cov^\bbC(z) :=\sum_{a\in A} |f^\bbC_a(z)|^2 \lambda^\bbC_a(z) :=\frac{|f^\bbC_a(z)|^2}{\cov^\bbC(z)} \Phi^\bbC(z) :=\log\left(\cov^\bbC(z)\right) \mu^\bbC(z) :=\sum_{a\in A} \lambda^\bbC_a(z)\, a.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-768: We want to prove the following.
elements.sent-2411-770: In particular, if $A\subset \bbZ^n$, then for all $V\subset\bbR^n$, we have \begin{equation}\esol{\Exp^\bbC;V\times i (-\pi,\pi)}=n!\vol_n\left(\mu^\bbC(V)\right).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-771: Which gives in the case $V=\bbR^n$: \begin{equation}\esol{\Exp^\bbC;\bbR^n\times i (-\pi,\pi)}=n!\vol_n(P).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-772: We will of course apply the general theory introduced in \cref{sec:KR .. We let \begin{equation}\scrF:=\Span_\bbC\set{f^\bbC_a}{a\in A}=\Span_\bbR\set{f^\bbC_a,i f^\bbC_a}{a\in A}\subset C^\infty(\bbC^n,\bbC).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-773: We endow this space with the Hermitian product such that $\{f^\bbC_a\}_{a\in A}$ is a unitary basis that we denote $(\cdot,\cdot)$.
elements.sent-2411-775: Note that this is now a complex hyperplane and thus of real codimension $2$.
elements.sent-2411-776: However the situation remains similar as in the real case, in pareticular, we have the following.
elements.sent-2411-778: For every $F:\scrF_z\to \bbR$ measurable, we have \begin{equation}\int_{\scrF_z} F(f) \rho_{\Exp^\bbC}(f)\, \mrd f=\frac{1}{\pi}\bbE[F(\calG_z)]\end{equation} where $\rho_{\Exp^\bbC (f) :=(1/\pi^n)\exp(-|f|^2)$ is the density of $\Exp^\bbC\randin \scrF$ and where \begin{equation}\label{eq:defGz} \calG_z:=\Exp^\bbC-\frac{\Exp^\bbC(z)}{\cov^\bbC(z)}\cov^\bbC(z,\cdot)\randin \scrF_z\end{equation} with \begin{equation}\cov^\bbC(z,z'):=\sum_{a\in A}\overline{f^\bbC_a(z)}\, f^\bbC_a(z'), \quad \forall z'\in\bbC^n.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-779: First of all, since $\cov^\bbC(z)=\cov^\bbC(z,z)$, one checks that $\calG_z(z)=0$ almost surely, i.e, $\calG_z\in \scrF_z$ almost surely.
elements.sent-2411-780: In fact we have \begin{equation}\calG_z=\pi_z(\Exp^\bbC)\end{equation} where $\pi_z:\scrF\to\scrF_z$ is the orthogonal projection.
elements.sent-2411-781: Indeed, as in the real case, one sees that $\left(\cov(z,\cdot),f\right)=f(z)$ for any $f\in\scrF$, i.e. $\cov^\bbC(z,\cdot)$ is the vector representing the complex linear form $ev^\bbC_z:f\mapsto f(z)$.
elements.sent-2411-782: Moreover one sees that $|ev^\bbC_z|^2=\cov^\bbC(z)$ and thus $\pi_z(f):=f-\frac{f(z) {\cov^\bbC(z)}\cov^\bbC(z,\cdot)\randin \scrF_z$ as claimed.}
elements.sent-2411-783: The rest of the proof is straightforward: \begin{equation}\frac{1}{\pi}\int_{\scrF_z} F(f) \frac{e^{|f|^2}}{\pi^{n-1}}\, \mrd f =\frac{1}{\pi}\int_{\scrF} F(\pi_z(f)) \frac{e^{|f|^2}}{\pi^{n}}\, \mrd f =\frac{1}{\pi}\bbE\left[F\left(\pi_z(\Exp^\bbC)\right) \right]=\frac{1}{\pi}\bbE\left[F\left(\calG_z\right) \right]\end{equation} which is what we wanted.
elements.sent-2411-784: Considering $\scrF$ as a real vector space, we have two real linear forms: \begin{equation}ev^R_z:f\mapsto \Re(f(z)) \mathand ev^I_z:f\mapsto \Im(f(z)).\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-788: Moreover, let us consider the endomorphism $J:\Hom_\bbR(\scrF,\bbR)\to \Hom_\bbR(\scrF,\bbR)$ given for all $\chi\in \Hom_\bbR(\scrF,\bbR)$ and $f\in \scrF$ by \begin{equation}J\chi(f):=\chi(if).\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-791: Since the complex structure $J$ preserves the scalar product we get \begin{equation}\|ev^R_z\wedge ev_z^I\|=\|ev^R_z\wedge J ev_z^R\|=\|ev_z^R\|^2=\sum_{a\in A}\Re\left(f^\bbC_a(z)\right)^2+\Im\left(f^\bbC_a(z)\right)^2=\cov^\bbC(z)\end{equation} which is what we wanted.
elements.sent-2411-792: We will also need the following computation.
